A global cache statically populated with "a-zA-Z0-9" would still speed up english text processing and require neglegible memory. A well-populated static cache for east-asian languages would probably require a significant amount of memory ( O(10)KB ). A dynamic global cache would IMHO not be a very good idea, as in python `b'\xF0\xBF\xBF\xBF'.decode('utf-8')` decodes just fine to some very large, unused unicode codepoint and thus an attacker might attempt a memory exhaustion attack by spamming this cache with large, invalid code points.

A quick test in a python shell gives me about 50MB for a 1M entry width-dict, which at about 50byte/entry looks reasonable. That means such a cache could only be used with some culling strategy, and I'm not aware of a really fast way to do that to dicts in python. Actually, I think some form of size-limiting would be necessary even in case of the call-local cache to prevent this kind of attack. I'll do some tests on that later.

I think the call-local cache is quite neat in that it won't affect (already short) computation times for short strings by much, but it still speeds up computation time for long strings significantly while maintaining an OK memory footprint during the call.

This isn't a hot code path, but given the choice to add explicit number casting here, I figured I'd run some benches anyway. I was expecting to conclude how much faster it is. Especially in Chrome given the history of optimising for asmjs and more generally with the high "type" confidence.

To my surprise, it turned the other way around. Implicit number casting consistently performs _faster_ than explicit number casting. Up to 40X even.

* the string UNSIGNED-PAYLOAD.

This is in a category of route/action handling that would be great to approach more systematically than we currently are. At a high level, I think we have a need for middleware wrappers around routing actions to handle cases like:

* 'not found'-type issues (e.g. content URLs, reports for invalid IDs, etc)
* authorization issues (e.g. loading a manage page as a regular user)
* general uncaught errors (current fallback on `SET_ERROR`

A lot of these are kind of the client-side version of what you'd expect to see from a properly configured web server (404s, 403s, 500s...) and we might be able to think about handling them similarly (e.g. adding "decorators" to various actions specifying what kind of user it's applicable for)

In general, I'd like to avoid redirecting for any of these because it dissociates the URL that caused the issue with the result. It means if someone wants to e.g. refresh the page to try again, it won't work. Instead, we should move towards incorporating common scenarios into `core-base` and handling the scenarios consistently across routing actions.

App units rely on etcd to resolve registry host and port.  If etcd isn't running locally, ideally `ETCDCTL_PEERS` is set to enable `etcdctl` to use _remote_ etcds for that purpose.

The idea was that anyone wanting to run their data plane in an elastic pool of machines _not_ running etcd (which seems a semi-advanced use case) could use cloud-config to poke a value for `ETCDCTL_PEERS` into `/etc/environment`.

Since there is possibly a security concern here, what if I kept this approach, but used cloud-config to poke `ETCDCTL_PEERS` into a _different_ file instead, like `/etc/app-environment`?  It would lack anything potentially sensitive or ripe for abuse like `COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4`.

Forthcoming documentation would illustrate how `ETCDCTL_PEERS` can be poked into that file using cloud-config.

As I said in the description the PR I think that this greatly improves understanding for developers using the API.

> connectionCb = new Callback()

We are creating a new instance of Callback for each call and perform passing of this callback to c function. JVM can't manage reference counting in C code. As result, GC will free this object on the next GC iteration and it will cause bad behavior like crashes or missed callback calls. 

To avoid this I suggest the following:

1. For each call create one static callback instance and `static Map<i32 /* command handle */, Future>`
2. Use global atomic counter to generate new command handles
3. When user calls our method generate new command handles and put corresponded Future to static Map
4. When libsovrin calls our callback resolve corresponded future with result
